body_txt: A popular literary archetype: a character who knows that treasure is out there in the world — whether it's been lost to time, or was actively hidden in a secret location — and is making it their mission to uncover as much of it as they can . They may be in for a menagerie of reasons why; maybe it's to get filthy rich, maybe it's for the glory , maybe it's to preserve it as an archeological artifact , maybe it's because it poses some kind of danger or brings some kind of great benefit . Whatever the case, pursuing these treasures will likely be a difficult, risky endeavor that poses great danger , so these characters are often greatly motivated, and will usually be throwing themselves towards adventure as often as the adventure comes to them. Treasure hunters are closely associated with Bold Explorers , Adventurer Archaeologists , similar archetypes who are defined by their desires to travel and uncover seemingly impossible things on their own, such as unknown lands or the remnants of ancient civilizations. Expect a lot of overlap, though also expect treasure hunters to be far more interested in, and thus defined by, the actual treasure/artifacts themselves and why they're considered valuable. Pirates are also a frequent archetype of overlap, what with them being thieves who travel far and wide for places to plunder and ill-gotten gains to be fought over. Classic adventure stories like Treasure Island coined further tropes to make "pirates" and "treasure hunters" more synonymous, namely the ideas of pirates of legend burying their treasure , prompting a massive race among various factions to claim it — expect a Friendly Pirate to be characterized more for their desire to find this loot as a way to not need them to participate in actual piracy . Just like the Adventurer Archaeologist , the lives of treasure hunters in fiction are often depicted as far more exciting and glamorous than their real-life counterparts. Many exotic tropes commonly associated with the treasure hunt from fiction — including the archetypal hunt with a Treasure Map leading to Pirate Booty — largely originate from exactly that: fiction. Real-world experts in "treasure hunting" were/are usually partnered with archeologists to assist in excavation sites to safely claim artifacts for legitimate study and preservation, or — as are most associated with shipwreck salvors — finding and retrieving known, but lost artifacts amidst wreckage otherwise almost impossible to locate, usually through very slow, careful, laborious means. Both in fiction and real life, self-professed "treasure hunters" tend to be viewed less wholesomely than archeologists since there's less of a pretense for operating in the better interest of society as a whole, and more for satisfying their Greed . From as early as the 13th century , it was known for some supposed archeological expeditions to be fraudulent as a means for treasure hunters to run off with money from their rich sponsors, and treasure hunters who don't respect the historical, archeological value of their discoveries beyond supposed monetary value can cause irreparable damage (see Heinrich Schliemann , the rich amateur archeologist who sought the historical city of Troy, successfully identified it, then proceeded to blow most of it up with TNT ). As a result, for more heroic Treasure Hunters, it's a common trend for the treasure to stop mattering to their main mission, and will often end up leaving it alone for one reason or another . In the modern age, with increasingly capable technology and tighter legal restrictions making it more difficult to claim discovered treasure as your own (nations like the US and UK have federal and parliamentary laws prohibiting certain actions upon uncovering treasure troves), the fantasy of traveling to an unexplored world and claiming some shiny lost loot for yourself has become decidedly less common, but in the land of fiction and fantasy, as long as there are treasures to be discovered, you better believe there will still be characters going after them. This proclivity for people to discover and nab loot manifests in a unique way in world of gaming: in addition to featuring the archetype as characters in-universe, many Tabletop RPGs like Dungeons & Dragons were built around players Dungeon Crawling to uncover all sorts of juicy plunder, and this habit has found its way into almost every other adventure-RPG in Tabletop and Video Games where players are encouraged (implicitly or otherwise) to nick whatever isn't nailed down . Players who are especially dedicated towards finding loot tend to be also nicknamed "treasure hunters" in their own regard: see Player Archetypes for more on that particular definition. See also Beachcombing , a very modern, often less sensationalized practice of treasure hunting through metal detectors (tools that, in more advanced and capable builds, are very useful for professional archeologists, but are often sold on the cheap to amateur treasure-hunting enthusiasts for hobby purposes).
